title: Pumpkin scones
subtitle: Spiced pumpkin scones with a soft, dense crumb
excerpt: Pureed cooked pumpkin takes the place of most of the liquid in these scones, producing a denser, softer crumb than a standard British scone. Pumpkin spice runs through the dough. They are fragrant, lightly sweet, and hold well for a day. Good with plain butter.
sourceNotes: Pumpkin scones are most commonly associated with Australian and American home baking, where they appear as a seasonal autumn preparation. The Australian version, associated with Queensland baking tradition, is particularly well known. This recipe follows that tradition of using cooked, pureed pumpkin as both a flavour component and a moisture source in place of dairy liquid.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze once baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen at 180 degrees C fan for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pumpkin can be cooked and pureed up to 2 days ahead and kept refrigerated. The scones are best eaten the day they are made.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; slightly lower than a plain scone because the pumpkin adds moisture

glossaryTerms.definition: Pumpkin puree adds moisture and some fat to the scone dough, producing a softer, denser crumb than a plain or fruit scone. The dough will feel stickier than a standard scone dough and is harder to cut cleanly. Work with lightly floured hands, do not overwork it, and accept that these scones are less layered and more muffin-like in texture. That is the correct result.

content.text: These are not classic British scones: the pumpkin puree makes the
dough stickier and the crumb denser
. Handle the dough as little as possible. Cook the pumpkin until completely soft before pureeing; any remaining texture will leave lumps in the scone.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Combine 250 g flour, 2 tsp baking powder, 0.25 tsp salt, and 1.5 tsp pumpkin spice in a bowl. Rub in 50 g cold butter to rough breadcrumbs. Stir in 50 g caster sugar. Whisk together 200 g pumpkin puree, 1 egg, and 50 ml buttermilk. Add the wet ingredients to the flour mixture and mix with a table knife until the dough just comes together. It will be softer and stickier than a plain scone dough.

Turn out onto a well-floured surface with floured hands. Pat gently to about 3 cm thick. Cut into rounds with a 6 cm cutter or into wedges with a knife. Place on the lined tray. Brush the tops with a little extra beaten egg or buttermilk. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until firm and golden. Cool on a wire rack for 10 minutes before serving.

items.problem: Dough is too wet to shape
items.solution: The pumpkin puree was too watery. Drain it through a sieve or a piece of muslin to remove excess liquid before weighing. Different pumpkin varieties vary significantly in water content.
The pumpkin itself has a mild flavour. Increase the pumpkin spice, or add 0.5 tsp of ground ginger and a pinch of nutmeg in addition to the spice blend.
